assumptions: The question concerns consolidated financial statements, not separate or individual financial statements.
The intragroup dividend has been declared so that an intragroup receivable/payable is recognised.
The risk being considered is foreign currency risk between group entities that may have different functional currencies.
No facts indicate that the group is an investment entity or that the intragroup dividend affects consolidated profit or loss through a related external transaction.

approaches.label: General prohibition on intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items must generally involve an external party. An intragroup dividend receivable is an intragroup item, so it is barred unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: The recorded intragroup dividend receivable cannot be designated in consolidation under the general rule.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to external-party items, with intragroup items generally excluded.

approaches.label: Exception for intragroup monetary items with non-eliminated FX exposure
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A recognised dividend receivable/payable may fit this route if it is a monetary item.
conditions: The declared dividend creates an intragroup monetary receivable/payable
The counterparties have different functional currencies
The resulting FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedge relationship is formally designated and documented
The hedge effectiveness criteria in IFRS 9 are met
approaches.practical_implication: A documented FX hedge may be possible, but only for the non-fully-eliminated foreign currency exposure on the intragroup monetary balance.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item in consolidation when FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness for any qualifying hedge relationship.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction cash flow hedge exception
approaches.reasoning: This exception applies to highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. A recognised dividend receivable is not a forecast transaction, and the context gives no basis that intragroup dividends affect consolidated profit or loss.
approaches.practical_implication: The forecast-transaction exception does not support hedging a recorded intragroup dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows forecast intragroup transactions to qualify only if denominated in another currency and the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraphs B6.3.5 and B6.3.6 explain that only forecast intragroup transactions affecting consolidated profit or loss qualify, with examples focused on onward external effects.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge alternative
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 addresses hedges of net investments in foreign operations, where the hedged item is an amount of net assets. That is a different hedge model from hedging an intragroup dividend receivable itself.
approaches.practical_implication: Only if the economic objective is to hedge the net investment, rather than the dividend receivable, would this model be relevant.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge type.
Paragraphs 2, 10, 11 and 12 limit this model to foreign currency risk on net investments, measured by reference to net assets and functional currencies.
Paragraphs 14 and 15 require IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements for a qualifying net investment hedge.
